│ 🚀 DeepEval Evaluation Results                                               │

╭──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────╮
│                                                                              │
│  ✅ test_case_0                                                              │
│  ├──   Input:              What is the GitLab team handbook used for?        │
│  │     Actual Output:      The GitLab team handbook is the central           │
│  │                         repository for how GitLab operates as a           │
│  │                         company. It serves as the single source of        │
│  │                         truth for processes, policies, product            │
│  │                         direction, strategy, and vision. It is used by    │
│  │                         GitLab team members to perform their jobs, by     │
│  │                         leadership for internal alignment and external    │
│  │                         stakeholder communications, and by potential      │
│  │                         applicants to understand role expectations,       │
│  │                         compensation and benefits, company values, and    │
│  │                         other policies. Additionally, it functions as     │
│  │                         a talent acquisition tool and an open resource    │
│  │                         for the broader community—offering                │
│  │                         transparency and inspiration for other            │
│  │                         organizations. The handbook is entirely           │
│  │                         public, reflecting GitLab’s value of              │
│  │                         transparency, and is distinct from user-facing    │
│  │                         or community code contribution documentation,     │
│  │                         which resides elsewhere.                          │
│  │     Expected Output:    The GitLab team handbook is the central           │
│  │                         repository for how GitLab runs the company,       │
│  │                         and it is open for feedback and merge             │
│  │                         requests.                                         │
│  └── Metrics                                                                 │
│       Status ┃ Metric             ┃ Score ┃ Threshold ┃ Reason               │
│      ━━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━  │

│                                                                              │
│  ✅ test_case_1                                                              │
│  ├──   Input:              Why does GitLab say the handbook helps            │
│  │                         onboarding and teamwork?                          │
│  │     Actual Output:      GitLab states that the handbook helps             │
│  │                         onboarding because “you can find all relevant     │
│  │                         information spelled out,” making it easier for    │
│  │                         new team members to quickly access and            │
│  │                         understand essential processes, policies, and     │
│  │                         expectations. It supports teamwork because        │
│  │                         “you can read how other parts of the company      │
│  │                         work,” enabling cross-functional collaboration    │
│  │                         and shared understanding across distributed       │
│  │                         teams.                                            │
│  │     Expected Output:    GitLab says the handbook helps because people     │
│  │                         can find relevant information in writing,         │
│  │                         understand how other parts of the company         │
│  │                         work, and propose improvements through merge      │
│  │                         requests.                                         │
│  └── Metrics                                                                 │
│       Status ┃ Metric             ┃ Score ┃ Threshold ┃ Reason               │

│                                                                              │
│  ✅ test_case_2                                                              │
│  ├──   Input:              How can someone contribute improvements to the    │
│  │                         GitLab Handbook?                                  │
│  │     Actual Output:      Someone can contribute improvements to the        │
│  │                         GitLab Handbook by submitting merge requests      │
│  │                         to the                                            │
│  │                         (https://gitlab.com/gitlab-com/content-sites/…    │
│  │                         It is encouraged to make **small, focused         │
│  │                         merge requests**, ideally merged on the same      │
│  │                         day to avoid conflicts and keep changes           │
│  │                         manageable. Contributors—whether inside or        │
│  │                         outside the company—are welcome to propose        │
│  │                         significant changes; suggestions can be           │
│  │                         discussed openly, and if agreed upon, merged.     │
│  │                         If someone makes a good suggestion, they are      │
│  │                         invited to add it directly to the handbook,       │
│  │                         with support offered (e.g., providing the         │
│  │                         relevant page URL or helping submit the change    │
│  │                         if needed). For guidance on licensing and         │
│  │                         contribution requirements, contributors should    │
│  │                         refer to the                                      │
│  │                         (https://gitlab.com/gitlab-com/content-sites/…    │
│  │     Expected Output:    Someone can contribute by submitting merge        │
│  │                         requests to the handbook project, making          │
│  │                         proposals, and participating in a welcoming       │
│  │                         contribution process.                             │

│                                                                              │
│  ✅ test_case_4                                                              │
│  ├──   Input:              Where should someone look if they want to         │
│  │                         learn how to edit the handbook?                   │
│  │     Actual Output:      Someone who wants to learn how to edit the        │
│  │                         handbook should start with the **"Editing the     │
│  │                         Handbook"** page, which focuses specifically      │
│  │                         on how to make edits. This page includes          │
│  │                         guidance on using the Web IDE—such as             │
│  │                         navigating to a handbook page, clicking “Edit     │
│  │                         this page”, and understanding the Web IDE         │
│  │                         interface (file tree, editing view, tabs, and     │
│  │                         status bar information). It also references       │
│  │                         complementary resources like the                  │
│  │                         (../handbook-usage.md) for context on why and     │
│  │                         how the handbook is used, and [Using GitLab at    │
│  │                         GitLab](/handbook/people-group) for broader       │
│  │                         practices.                                        │
│  │                                                                           │
│  │                         Additionally, practical tips—including            │
│  │                         AI-assisted editing with Anthropic Claude and     │
│  │                         GitLab Duo—are covered in both the main           │
│  │                         editing page and the **"Practical Handbook        │
│  │                         Editing Tips"** page, which serves as a           │
│  │                         supplement for non-technical team members.        │
│  │     Expected Output:    They should use the editing handbook page for     │
│  │                         how to edit the handbook, while the handbook      │
│  │                         usage page explains how and why GitLab uses       │
│  │                         it.                                               │

│  ├──   Input:              Which handbook issues should only be escalated    │
│  │                         according to the handbook escalation page?        │
│  │     Actual Output:      According to the handbook escalation page,        │
│  │                         issues should **only be escalated** if they       │
│  │                         relate to:                                        │
│  │                                                                           │
│  │                         1. The default branch being broken                │
│  │                         2. Broken infrastructure                          │
│  │                         3. Time-sensitive updates to the handbook         │
│  │                         where there is an issue preventing the update     │
│  │                         from being made                                   │
│  │                                                                           │
│  │                         These are the only scenarios that warrant         │
│  │                         escalation, as explicitly stated in the           │
│  │                         documentation.                                    │
│  │     Expected Output:    Issues should only be escalated if they relate    │
│  │                         to the default branch being broken, broken        │
│  │                         infrastructure, or time sensitive updates to      │
│  │                         the handbook where there is an issue making       │
│  │                         the update.                                       │

│              │                    │       │           │ indicating perfect   │
│              │                    │       │           │ faithfulness.        │
│        PASS  │ Contextual         │ 0.50  │ 0.50      │ The score is 0.50    │
│              │ Precision          │       │           │ because the only     │
│              │                    │       │           │ relevant node is     │
│              │                    │       │           │ ranked second,       │
│              │                    │       │           │ while the first,     │
│              │                    │       │           │ third, fourth, and   │
│              │                    │       │           │ fifth nodes are      │
│              │                    │       │           │ irrelevant.          │
│              │                    │       │           │ Ideally, the         │
│              │                    │       │           │ relevant node        │
│              │                    │       │           │ should be ranked     │
│              │                    │       │           │ first to achieve a   │
│              │                    │       │           │ higher score. The    │
│              │                    │       │           │ first node is        │
│              │                    │       │           │ irrelevant because   │
│              │                    │       │           │ it discusses when    │
│              │                    │       │           │ to hand over to      │
│              │                    │       │           │ Reliability          │
│              │                    │       │           │ Engineering but      │
│              │                    │       │           │ does not list the    │
│              │                    │       │           │ specific             │
│              │                    │       │           │ escalation           │
│              │                    │       │           │ conditions. The      │
│              │                    │       │           │ second node is       │
│              │                    │       │           │ relevant as it       │
│              │                    │       │           │ explicitly states    │
│              │                    │       │           │ the conditions for   │
│              │                    │       │           │ escalation. The      │
│              │                    │       │           │ third node is        │
│              │                    │       │           │ irrelevant as it     │
│              │                    │       │           │ describes            │
│              │                    │       │           │ expectations for     │
│              │                    │       │           │ the 'Keep main       │
│              │                    │       │           │ green' group         │
│              │                    │       │           │ without addressing   │
│              │                    │       │           │ escalation           │
│              │                    │       │           │ conditions. The      │
│              │                    │       │           │ fourth node is       │
│              │                    │       │           │ irrelevant because   │
│              │                    │       │           │ it provides          │
│              │                    │       │           │ general guidance     │
│              │                    │       │           │ on reporting         │
│              │                    │       │           │ issues and Slack     │
│              │                    │       │           │ channels. The        │
│              │                    │       │           │ fifth node is        │
│              │                    │       │           │ irrelevant as it     │
│              │                    │       │           │ discusses managing   │
│              │                    │       │           │ broken main alerts   │
│              │                    │       │           │ and CI pipelines,    │
│              │                    │       │           │ not the specific     │
│              │                    │       │           │ escalation           │
│              │                    │       │           │ criteria.            │
│        PASS  │ Contextual Recall  │ 1.00  │ 0.50      │ The score is 1.00    │
│              │                    │       │           │ because the single   │
│              │                    │       │           │ sentence in the      │
│              │                    │       │           │ expected output is   │
│              │                    │       │           │ fully supported by   │
│              │                    │       │           │ the 2nd node in      │
│              │                    │       │           │ the retrieval        │
│              │                    │       │           │ context, which       │
│              │                    │       │           │ lists the exact      │
│              │                    │       │           │ same conditions      │
│              │                    │       │           │ for escalation.      │
│                                                                              │
╰──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────╯
╭──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────╮
│                                                                              │
│  ❌ test_case_8                                                              │
│  ├──   Input:              What is the general flow for changing a           │
│  │                         process in the GitLab handbook?                   │
│  │     Actual Output:      The general flow for changing a process in the    │
│  │                         GitLab handbook is:                               │
│  │                                                                           │
│  │                         1. A process problem arises—often surfaced in     │
│  │                         an issue or chat.                                 │
│  │                         2. A proposal to address it is made **directly    │
│  │                         as a merge request (MR)** to the handbook (not    │
│  │                         via email, Google Doc, or issue description).     │
│  │                         3. The MR should clearly state the change,        │
│  │                         include the *why* (e.g., business goal or         │
│  │                         inspiration), and mention affected people. For    │
│  │                         limited tests, add a note indicating the          │
│  │                         experimental nature; remove it once the test      │
│  │                         concludes and the process is fully adopted.       │
│  │                         4. The MR is reviewed and merged—ideally          │
│  │                         quickly, with appropriate team members (e.g.,     │
│  │                         function-specific CODEOWNERS or                   │
│  │                         cross-functional approvers like the CMO or        │
│  │                         CEO/Chief of Staff) engaged.                      │
│  │                         5. After merging, the change is announced:        │
│  │                            - Major changes go to the                      │
│  │                         `#whats-happening-at-gitlab` Slack channel,       │
│  │                            - Medium changes are posted in `#handbook`     │
│  │                         with a one-line summary,                          │
│  │                            - Any related issue is closed with a link      │
│  │                         to the MR diff or commit.                         │
│  │                                                                           │
│  │                         Crucially, **the handbook *is* the process**:     │
│  │                         updating it *is* changing the process—not just    │
│  │                         documenting it.                                   │
│  │     Expected Output:    A process problem comes up, a proposal is made    │
│  │                         in a handbook merge request, and once merged      │
│  │                         the change is announced by linking to the diff    │
│  │                         or commit.                                        │
│  └── Metrics                                                                 │
│       Status ┃ Metric             ┃ Score ┃ Threshold ┃ Reason               │
│      ━━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━  │
│        PASS  │ Answer Relevancy   │ 1.00  │ 0.50      │ The score is 1.00    │
│              │                    │       │           │ because the actual   │
│              │                    │       │           │ output contains no   │
│              │                    │       │           │ irrelevant           │
│              │                    │       │           │ statements and       │
│              │                    │       │           │ fully addresses      │
│              │                    │       │           │ the input question   │
│              │                    │       │           │ about the general    │
│              │                    │       │           │ flow for changing    │
│              │                    │       │           │ a process in the     │
│              │                    │       │           │ GitLab handbook.     │
│        PASS  │ Faithfulness       │ 1.00  │ 0.50      │ The score is 1.00    │
│              │                    │       │           │ because there are    │
│              │                    │       │           │ no contradictions    │
│              │                    │       │           │ between the actual   │
│              │                    │       │           │ output and the       │
│              │                    │       │           │ retrieval context,   │
│              │                    │       │           │ indicating perfect   │
│              │                    │       │           │ faithfulness.        │
│        FAIL  │ Contextual         │ 0.25  │ 0.50      │ The score is 0.25    │
│              │ Precision          │       │           │ because the only     │
│              │                    │       │           │ relevant node is     │
│              │                    │       │           │ ranked fourth,       │
│              │                    │       │           │ while the first      │
│              │                    │       │           │ three nodes are      │
│              │                    │       │           │ irrelevant. The      │
│              │                    │       │           │ first node is        │
│              │                    │       │           │ irrelevant because   │
│              │                    │       │           │ it 'does not         │
│              │                    │       │           │ explicitly mention   │
│              │                    │       │           │ the full flow of     │
│              │                    │       │           │ problem, proposal,   │
│              │                    │       │           │ announcement.' The   │
│              │                    │       │           │ second node is       │
│              │                    │       │           │ irrelevant because   │
│              │                    │       │           │ it 'does not cover   │
│              │                    │       │           │ the initial          │
│              │                    │       │           │ problem or the       │
│              │                    │       │           │ announcement         │
│              │                    │       │           │ step.' The third     │
│              │                    │       │           │ node is irrelevant   │
│              │                    │       │           │ because it 'does     │
│              │                    │       │           │ not include the      │
│              │                    │       │           │ initial problem      │
│              │                    │       │           │ step.' The fourth    │
│              │                    │       │           │ node is relevant     │
│              │                    │       │           │ as it 'directly      │
│              │                    │       │           │ states the full      │
│              │                    │       │           │ flow.' The fifth     │
│              │                    │       │           │ node is also         │
│              │                    │       │           │ irrelevant. Since    │
│              │                    │       │           │ the relevant node    │
│              │                    │       │           │ appears after        │
│              │                    │       │           │ three irrelevant     │
│              │                    │       │           │ ones, the            │
│              │                    │       │           │ precision at         │
│              │                    │       │           │ higher ranks is      │
│              │                    │       │           │ low, resulting in    │
│              │                    │       │           │ a score of 0.25.     │
│        PASS  │ Contextual Recall  │ 1.00  │ 0.50      │ The score is 1.00    │
│              │                    │       │           │ because every        │
│              │                    │       │           │ sentence in the      │
│              │                    │       │           │ expected output is   │
│              │                    │       │           │ fully supported by   │
│              │                    │       │           │ the 4th node in      │
│              │                    │       │           │ the retrieval        │
│              │                    │       │           │ context, with no     │
│              │                    │       │           │ unsupported          │
│              │                    │       │           │ content. Great       │
│              │                    │       │           │ job!                 │
│                                                                              │
╰──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────╯
╭──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────╮
│                                                                              │
│  ✅ test_case_9                                                              │
│  ├──   Input:              What is GitLab's key goal when pursuing           │
│  │                         acquisitions?                                     │
│  │     Actual Output:      GitLab's key goal in pursuing acquisitions is     │
│  │                         to accelerate its roadmap and offer better        │
│  │                         tools to customers more quickly. Specifically,    │
│  │                         GitLab aims to introduce new product              │
│  │                         categories and mature early-stage categories      │
│  │                         faster than it could by building everything       │
│  │                         internally. This is achieved by acquiring         │
│  │                         strong teams that have already built great        │
│  │                         tools and products relevant to GitLab             │
│  │                         customers—and integrating their technology        │
│  │                         into GitLab.                                      │
│  │     Expected Output:    GitLab's key goal in pursuing acquisitions is     │
│  │                         to accelerate the roadmap and offer better        │
│  │                         tools to customers more quickly.                  │
│  └── Metrics                                                                 │
│       Status ┃ Metric             ┃ Score ┃ Threshold ┃ Reason               │

│              │                    │       │           │ the input question   │

│              │                    │       │           │ acquisitions.        │
│        PASS  │ Faithfulness       │ 1.00  │ 0.50      │ The score is 1.00    │
│              │                    │       │           │ because there are    │
│              │                    │       │           │ no contradictions    │
│              │                    │       │           │ between the actual   │

│              │                    │       │           │ retrieval context,   │
│              │                    │       │           │ indicating perfect   │
│              │                    │       │           │ faithfulness.        │
│        PASS  │ Contextual         │ 1.00  │ 0.50      │ The score is 1.00    │

│              │                    │       │           │ ranked first, and    │

│              │                    │       │           │ nodes are ranked     │
│              │                    │       │           │ after it, which is   │
│              │                    │       │           │ exactly how a        │

│              │                    │       │           │ precision score is   │

│                                                                              │
│  ✅ test_case_10                                                             │
│  ├──   Input:              What should a team member do if the handbook      │
│  │                         escalation channel does not receive a             │
│  │                         response?                                         │
│  │     Actual Output:      If a team member posts in the                     │
│  │                         `#handbook-escalation` channel and does not       │
│  │                         receive a response, they should:                  │
│  │                                                                           │
│  │                         - Ping the (maintenance.md#team-structure) if     │
│  │                         it is within their working hours.                 │
│  │                         - If the handbook DRIs are unavailable, they      │
│  │                         may ping members of the ["keep main green"        │
│  │                         group](maintenance.md#team-structure), who        │
│  │                         have volunteered to assist when available.        │
│  │                         - When reaching out, the team member should       │
│  │                         consider the recipient’s timezone, working        │
│  │                         hours (as indicated in their Slack profile),      │
│  │                         and Slack status (e.g., whether they are          │
│  │                         marked as out of office).                         │
│  │                                                                           │
│  │                         Additionally, any GitLab team member can          │
│  │                         volunteer to join `#handbook-escalation` to       │
│  │                         help — interested individuals can ask someone     │
│  │                         already in the group to add them.                 │
│  │     Expected Output:    If a team member does not receive a response      │
│  │                         after posting in #handbook-escalation, they       │
│  │                         should ping the handbook DRIs if it is within     │
│  │                         their working hours.                              │
│  └── Metrics                                                                 │
│       Status ┃ Metric             ┃ Score ┃ Threshold ┃ Reason               │
│      ━━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━  │

│              │                    │       │           │ output contains no   │
│              │                    │       │           │ irrelevant           │
│              │                    │       │           │ statements and       │
│              │                    │       │           │ fully addresses      │
│              │                    │       │           │ the input            │

│              │                    │       │           │ channel requires     │
│              │                    │       │           │ being added by       │

│              │                    │       │           │ can volunteer to     │

│              │                    │       │           │ contradiction, but   │
│              │                    │       │           │ the rest of the      │
│              │                    │       │           │ output is faithful   │
│              │                    │       │           │ to the context.      │

│              │                    │       │           │ all subsequent       │

│              │                    │       │           │ is the ideal         │
│              │                    │       │           │ ranking for          │

│                                                                              │
│  ✅ test_case_11                                                             │
│  ├──   Input:              Where should handbook contributors look for       │
│  │                         writing and style guidance?                       │
│  │     Actual Output:      Handbook contributors should consult the          │
│  │                         following resources for writing and style         │
│  │                         guidance:                                         │
│  │                                                                           │
│  │                         - The [Handbook Markdown                          │
│  │                         Guide](https://handbook.gitlab.com/docs/markd…    │
│  │                         - The [Editing Handbook                           │
│  │                         page](editing-handbook/_index.md#naming-pages…    │
│  │                         especially regarding naming pages and folder      │
│  │                         structure                                         │
│  │                         - GitLab’s [Writing Style                         │
│  │                         Guidelines](/handbook/communication/#writing-…    │
│  │                         - As a fallback, the [Documentation Style         │
│  │                         Guide](https://docs.gitlab.com/ee/development…    │
│  │                                                                           │
│  │                         These are explicitly cited as the primary         │
│  │                         sources of handbook-specific and general          │
│  │                         writing guidance.                                 │
│  │     Expected Output:    Contributors should use the handbook style        │
│  │                         guide and its related resources to keep           │
│  │                         handbook content consistent and easier to         │
│  │                         maintain.                                         │

│       Status ┃ Metric             ┃ Score ┃ Threshold ┃ Reason               │

│                                                                              │
│  ❌ test_case_12                                                             │
│  ├──   Input:              What are the five key stages in GitLab's          │
│  │                         acquisition process?                              │
│  │     Actual Output:      Based on the provided handbook context, the       │
│  │                         five key stages in GitLab's acquisition           │
│  │                         process are:                                      │
│  │                                                                           │
│  │                         1. **Early Diligence** – Includes initial         │
│  │                         technical calls, internal note-taking in the      │
│  │                         Technical Evaluation Google Doc, and              │
│  │                         refraining from requesting access to              │
│  │                         proprietary code at this stage.                   │
│  │                                                                           │
│  │                         2. **Confirmatory Due Diligence** – Involves      │
│  │                         deeper evaluation, including employee reviews     │
│  │                         and interviews, retention conversations for       │
│  │                         key technical employees, and final internal       │
│  │                         review leading to explicit executive approval     │
│  │                         (CLO, CFO, CEO).                                  │
│  │                                                                           │
│  │                         3. **Integration** – A post-transaction phase     │
│  │                         focused on product integration strategy,          │
│  │                         including decisions on which features to keep,    │
│  │                         rewrite, or discard/EOL, and identifying what     │
│  │                         is critical for user migration.                   │
│  │                                                                           │
│  │                         4. **Terminated Acquisition Engagements** –       │
│  │                         Applies when an acquisition is halted after       │
│  │                         the Technical Call; includes conducting a         │
│  │                         retrospective, archiving Slack channels, and      │
│  │                         removing diligence materials per NDA              │
│  │                         obligations.                                      │
│  │                                                                           │
│  │                         5. **Acquisition Team Responsibilities / Deal     │
│  │                         Execution** – Though not explicitly named as a    │
│  │                         standalone “stage”, the structured                │
│  │                         responsibilities across Business, Product,        │
│  │                         Engineering, and Finance teams—including          │
│  │                         creating the business case, outlining product     │
│  │                         implementation, performing technical              │
│  │                         diligence, and validating integration             │
│  │                         feasibility—constitute the core execution         │
│  │                         layer that bridges diligence to closing. This     │
│  │                         aligns with the formalized workflow implied by    │
│  │                         the “Acquisition Team” section and the            │
│  │                         “Conclusive call” and definitive agreement        │
│  │                         approvals in Confirmatory Due Diligence.          │
│  │                                                                           │
│  │                         Note: The handbook does not list exactly five     │
│  │                         *named* sequential stages outright; however,      │
│  │                         these five represent the distinct, documented     │
│  │                         phases with defined activities, deliverables,     │
│  │                         and ownership.                                    │
│  │     Expected Output:    The five key stages are Pipeline Building,        │
│  │                         Exploratory, Early Diligence, Confirmatory Due    │
│  │                         Diligence, and Integration.                       │
│  └── Metrics                                                                 │
│       Status ┃ Metric             ┃ Score ┃ Threshold ┃ Reason               │

│              │                    │       │           │ partially covers     │
│              │                    │       │           │ 'Early Diligence'    │
│              │                    │       │           │ and 'Integration',   │

│              │                    │       │           │ discusses            │

│              │                    │       │           │ mentions             │
│              │                    │       │           │ 'Confirmatory Due    │
│              │                    │       │           │ Diligence', and      │
│              │                    │       │           │ the fifth node       │
│              │                    │       │           │ mentions             │
│              │                    │       │           │ 'Confirmatory Due    │
│              │                    │       │           │ Diligence' and       │
│              │                    │       │           │ 'Early Diligence'    │
│              │                    │       │           │ but none list all    │
│              │                    │       │           │ five key stages.     │
│              │                    │       │           │ Since every node     │
│              │                    │       │           │ is irrelevant, the   │
│              │                    │       │           │ ranking order        │
│              │                    │       │           │ cannot improve the   │
│              │                    │       │           │ score.               │
│        FAIL  │ Contextual Recall  │ 0.00  │ 0.50      │ The score is 0.00    │
│              │                    │       │           │ because the          │
│              │                    │       │           │ expected output      │
│              │                    │       │           │ mentions 'Pipeline   │
│              │                    │       │           │ Building' and        │
│              │                    │       │           │ 'Exploratory'        │
│              │                    │       │           │ stages, which are    │
│              │                    │       │           │ not found in any     │
│              │                    │       │           │ node in the          │
│              │                    │       │           │ retrieval context.   │
│              │                    │       │           │ The context nodes    │
│              │                    │       │           │ only cover 'Early    │
│              │                    │       │           │ Diligence',          │
│              │                    │       │           │ 'Confirmatory Due    │

│                                                                              │
│  ✅ test_case_13                                                             │
│  ├──   Input:              What is the acquisition process communications    │
│  │                         page used for?                                    │
│  │     Actual Output:      The acquisition process communications page       │
│  │                         serves as a central resource for guiding how      │
│  │                         GitLab handles communications—both internal       │
│  │                         and external—throughout the acquisition           │
│  │                         lifecycle. It outlines responsibilities,          │
│  │                         decision-making frameworks, and key               │
│  │                         considerations before and after closing, such     │
│  │                         as:                                               │
│  │                                                                           │
│  │                         - Establishing dedicated communication            │
│  │                         channels (e.g., Slack) and assigning DRIs for     │
│  │                         each team and asset (e.g., blog post, press       │
│  │                         release, FAQ).                                    │
│  │                         - Defining media strategy (proactive vs.          │
│  │                         reactive), timing, key messaging, spokesperson    │
│  │                         designation, and notification plans for key       │
│  │                         stakeholders and EBAs.                            │
│  │                         - Directing responses to leaks or                 │
│  │                         rumors—including ownership by the                 │
│  │                         Communications Team, coordination with            │
│  │                         Legal/Finance/Accounting for regulatory           │
│  │                         compliance, and informing target company          │
│  │                         representatives.                                  │
│  │                         - Supporting coordination with the target         │
│  │                         company on employee and customer                  │
│  │                         communications, aligned with deal terms and       │
│  │                         transaction structure.                            │
│  │                         - Providing links to related processes, such      │
│  │                         as media request handling and analyst             │
│  │                         outreach.                                         │
│  │                                                                           │
│  │                         It is referenced across integration and           │
│  │                         corporate development workflows to ensure         │
│  │                         consistent, compliant, and strategic              │
│  │                         communication execution.                          │
│  │     Expected Output:    The acquisition process communications page       │
│  │                         documents communication practices related to      │
│  │                         an acquisition process, including how             │
│  │                         acquisition updates and related communications    │
│  │                         should be handled.                                │

│  ❌ test_case_14                                                             │
│  ├──   Input:              What does the acquisition integration page        │
│  │                         focus on?                                         │
│  │     Actual Output:      The acquisition integration page focuses on       │
│  │                         providing a detailed view of GitLab’s             │
│  │                         acquisition integration process. It covers key    │
│  │                         areas such as field enablement, sales and         │
│  │                         marketing messaging, and enablement               │
│  │                         development—including specific assets like        │
│  │                         prospect FAQs, overview and technical             │
│  │                         webcasts, and snapshot videos. It also            │
│  │                         outlines how the integration delivers value to    │
│  │                         users and impacts product, customers, and         │
│  │                         prospects. For broader context on GitLab’s        │
│  │                         overall acquisitions approach, it references      │
│  │                         the general acquisitions process page.            │
│  │     Expected Output:    The acquisition integration page focuses on       │
│  │                         the work after an acquisition, describing how     │
│  │                         an acquired company or team is integrated into    │
│  │                         GitLab.                                           │

│                                                                              │
│  ✅ test_case_15                                                             │
│  ├──   Input:              Which three Alliance Partner types does the       │
│  │                         GitLab Alliances Handbook describe?               │
│  │     Actual Output:      The GitLab Alliances Handbook describes three     │
│  │                         Alliance Partner types:                           │
│  │                                                                           │
│  │                         - **Cloud Partners**: Partners that provide       │
│  │                         public cloud computing services and software      │
│  │                         marketplaces where customers can procure and      │
│  │                         deploy GitLab.                                    │
│  │                         - **Platform Partners**: Partners that provide    │
│  │                         modern cloud / cloud-native application           │
│  │                         platforms which increase the modularity and       │
│  │                         extensibility of GitLab across enterprises and    │
│  │                         architectures.                                    │
│  │                         - **Technology Partners**: Independent            │
│  │                         Software Vendors (ISV) Partners with              │
│  │                         complementary technologies that seamlessly        │
│  │                         integrate or interoperate with GitLab for more    │
│  │                         complete customer solutions.                      │
│  │     Expected Output:    The GitLab Alliances Handbook describes Cloud     │
│  │                         Partners, Platform Partners, and Technology       │
│  │                         Partners as the three Alliance Partner types.     │
│  └── Metrics                                                                 │
│       Status ┃ Metric             ┃ Score ┃ Threshold ┃ Reason               │

│                                                                              │
│  ✅ test_case_16                                                             │
│  ├──   Input:              How does the alliances integration                │
│  │                         instructions page help technology partners?       │
│  │     Actual Output:      The alliances integration instructions page       │
│  │                         helps technology partners by guiding them         │
│  │                         through key processes to collaborate with         │
│  │                         GitLab, including:                                │
│  │                                                                           │
│  │                         - **Becoming a GitLab Technology Partner**: It    │
│  │                         outlines the final step—submitting a Merge        │
│  │                         Request—to get listed on the official             │
│  │                         [Technology Partners                              │
│  │                         page](https://about.gitlab.com/partners/techn…    │
│  │                         requiring a dedicated integration page,           │
│  │                         technical documentation, a screencast             │
│  │                         (preferred), a short description (≤314            │
│  │                         characters), a listed integration name, and a     │
│  │                         compliant logo.                                   │
│  │                                                                           │
│  │                         - **Setting up collaborative development          │
│  │                         environments**: Partners can request a joint      │
│  │                         private project in the Alliance group on          │
│  │                         GitLab.com, enabling them to create sandbox       │
│  │                         projects for hands-on testing, workflow           │
│  │                         validation, and public-facing demos—initially     │
│  │                         private, later convertible to public as           │
│  │                         needed.                                           │
│  │                                                                           │
│  │                         - **Adding their app to the Partners Page**:      │
│  │                         Step-by-step instructions are provided for        │
│  │                         forking the `www.gitlab.com` repository, using    │
│  │                         the Web IDE to edit `apps.yml`, uploading         │
│  │                         their logo following strict naming and            │
│  │                         formatting rules (e.g., filename must exactly     │
│  │                         match the app title in `apps.yml`), and           │
│  │                         committing changes.                               │
│  │                                                                           │
│  │                         - **Getting support**: Partners are directed      │
│  │                         to open issues in the [Alliance                   │
│  │                         project](https://gitlab.com/gitlab-com/allian…    │
│  │                         for assistance—whether strategic or               │
│  │                         technical—ensuring timely responses via           │
│  │                         GitLab’s issue-based workflow.                    │
│  │                                                                           │
│  │                         These instructions collectively empower           │
│  │                         partners to integrate technically, showcase       │
│  │                         their solution publicly, collaborate securely,    │
│  │                         and engage effectively with GitLab’s              │
│  │                         ecosystem.                                        │
│  │     Expected Output:    The alliances integration instructions page       │
│  │                         explains how a technology partner can get         │
│  │                         listed, add an app to the partners page,          │
│  │                         continue further development, and troubleshoot    │
│  │                         questions.                                        │
│  └── Metrics                                                                 │

│  ✅ test_case_19                                                             │
│  ├──   Input:              What kind of information is collected in the      │
│  │                         Business Technology infrastructure reference      │
│  │                         links page?                                       │
│  │     Actual Output:      The Business Technology infrastructure            │
│  │                         reference links page collects organizational      │
│  │                         and operational information related to            │
│  │                         infrastructure management, including:             │
│  │                                                                           │
│  │                         - **Repositories**: Links to GitLab               │
│  │                         repositories for Business Technology              │
│  │                         Engineering Infrastructure that do not contain    │
│  │                         sensitive information—hosted at                   │
│  │                         `gitlab.com/gitlab-com/business-technology/en…    │
│  │                         as opposed to sensitive infrastructure code       │
│  │                         hosted on `ops.gitlab.net` [1].                   │
│  │                                                                           │
│  │                         - **Labels and Tags**: Categorized labels for     │
│  │                         issue tracking and workflow management, such      │
│  │                         as:                                               │
│  │                           - `bt-infra-service::dns` for DNS-related       │
│  │                         domains, zones, and records [2],                  │
│  │                           - `bt-infra::access-request` for                │
│  │                         provisioning access requests [5],                 │
│  │                           - `bt-infra::bug` for bug reports and urgent    │
│  │                         fixes not tied to a specific code repository      │
│  │                         [3].                                              │
│  │                                                                           │
│  │                         - **Milestones**: Time-based planning markers     │
│  │                         (e.g., monthly milestones like “BT Infra          │
│  │                         2021-07”) used for lightweight time tracking,     │
│  │                         capacity planning, and roadmap alignment [4].     │
│  │                                                                           │
│  │                         These elements collectively support consistent    │
│  │                         issue triage, collaboration, and                  │
│  │                         infrastructure lifecycle management across the    │
│  │                         team.                                             │
│  │     Expected Output:    The infrastructure reference links page           │
│  │                         collects references such as milestones,           │
│  │                         repositories, labels, and tags used by the        │
│  │                         Business Technology engineering infrastructure    │
│  │                         area.                                             │

│  │                                                                           │

│  │                         management, compliance, and operational           │

│  ✅ test_case_22                                                             │
│  ├──   Input:              What kind of policy is documented in the          │
│  │                         GitLab business continuity plan page?             │
│  │     Actual Output:      The GitLab Business Continuity Plan documents     │
│  │                         a **policy for preventing and recovering from     │
│  │                         potential threats** to ensure personnel and       │
│  │                         assets remain protected and operational during    │
│  │                         business disruptions. It outlines procedures      │
│  │                         related to remote work contingencies, vendor      │
│  │                         communication and service restoration,            │
│  │                         escalation protocols for incidents, and           │
│  │                         testing of continuity plans—including             │
│  │                         scenarios like individual system outages. As      │
│  │                         GitLab is fully remote, the Incident Response     │
│  │                         Process forms the core of its Business            │
│  │                         Continuity Plan.                                  │
│  │     Expected Output:    The GitLab business continuity plan page          │
│  │                         documents continuity planning for business        │
│  │                         systems and enterprise application operations.    │

│  ├──   Input:              What does the Enterprise Applications division    │
│  │                         page describe?                                    │
│  │     Actual Output:      The Enterprise Applications division page         │
│  │                         describes the **IT Enterprise Applications        │
│  │                         team**, which oversees the business technology    │
│  │                         architecture and systems that power GitLab as     │
│  │                         a business entity. It emphasizes ensuring         │
│  │                         these systems are **fit-for-purpose,              │
│  │                         available, reliable, integrated, and secure**.    │
│  │                         The team focuses on building business-aligned     │
│  │                         technology to run operations, adding              │
│  │                         integrations and automations to support remote    │
│  │                         work, enhance team member and customer            │
│  │                         experience, and generate value. It also           │
│  │                         highlights the team’s diverse expertise in        │
│  │                         delivering high-quality solutions and support     │
│  │                         for internal customers, and directs readers to    │
│  │                         a directory listing for details on individual     │
│  │                         divisions, policies, and procedures.              │
│  │     Expected Output:    The Enterprise Applications division page         │
│  │                         describes the Enterprise Applications groups,     │
│  │                         their mission, teams, services, and operating     │
│  │                         model.                                            │

│                                                                              │
│  ❌ test_case_25                                                             │
│  ├──   Input:              What is the Enterprise Applications CRM team      │
│  │                         mission?                                          │
│  │     Actual Output:      The Enterprise Applications CRM team’s mission    │
│  │                         is to deliver a robust, end-to-end solution       │
│  │                         that streamlines and enhances the customer        │
│  │                         lifecycle at every stage, including:              │
│  │                                                                           │
│  │                         - **Lead Generation and Management:**             │
│  │                         Providing tools and automations that drive        │
│  │                         efficient lead capture, nurturing, and            │
│  │                         conversion.                                       │
│  │                         - **Sales Enablement:** Optimizing sales          │
│  │                         cycles through simplified workflows and           │
│  │                         actionable insights.                              │
│  │                         - **Subscription Management:** Ensuring a         │
│  │                         seamless quote-to-cash process with Zuora’s       │
│  │                         subscription and billing capabilities.            │
│  │                         - **Real-Time Insights:** Delivering              │
│  │                         data-driven insights that empower teams to        │
│  │                         make informed decisions and strengthen            │
│  │                         customer relationships.                           │
│  │                                                                           │
│  │                         Additionally, the team aims to:                   │
│  │                         - **Automate and Optimize** processes to          │
│  │                         eliminate manual bottlenecks and ensure           │
│  │                         efficiency and accuracy;                          │
│  │                         - **Scale with the Business**, building           │
│  │                         systems capable of supporting GitLab’s            │
│  │                         evolving needs as it grows;                       │
│  │                         - **Empower Teams**, equipping internal teams     │
│  │                         with tools and knowledge to build stronger        │
│  │                         customer connections, increase operational        │
│  │                         efficiency, and accelerate growth.                │
│  │                                                                           │
│  │                         They also commit to:                              │
│  │                         - **Strengthening Customer Relationships** by     │
│  │                         enabling teams to anticipate and exceed           │
│  │                         expectations;                                     │
│  │                         - **Enhancing Operational Efficiency** through    │
│  │                         smart automation and integrations;                │
│  │                         - **Accelerating Revenue Growth** via             │
│  │                         accurate, timely billing, seamless                │
│  │                         subscription management, and efficient deal       │
│  │                         closures;                                         │
│  │                         - **Driving Innovation** by continuously          │
│  │                         iterating and adopting industry best practices    │
│  │                         to maintain a cutting-edge CRM and billing        │
│  │                         ecosystem.                                        │
│  │     Expected Output:    The CRM team mission is to deliver a robust,      │
│  │                         end-to-end solution that streamlines and          │
│  │                         enhances the customer lifecycle at every          │
│  │                         stage.                                            │

│                                                                              │
│  ✅ test_case_26                                                             │
│  ├──   Input:              What does the Finsys access requests page         │
│  │                         cover?                                            │
│  │     Actual Output:      The Finsys access requests page covers            │
│  │                         system-specific access request requirements       │
│  │                         for various finance systems—including Zuora       │
│  │                         Billing, Zuora Revenue, Navan, NetSuite,          │
│  │                         Stripe, Avalara, and Coupa—detailing how          │
│  │                         roles, permissions, policies, and modules must    │
│  │                         be explicitly specified in each request. It       │
│  │                         also defines the “Definition of Done” for an      │
│  │                         access request from the FinSys perspective,       │
│  │                         requiring:                                        │
│  │                         - Clear identification of system-specific         │
│  │                         roles and permissions being requested,            │
│  │                         - Approvals from both the requester’s manager     │
│  │                         and the Business Owner, and                       │
│  │                         - A timestamped screenshot of the provisioned     │
│  │                         user record.                                      │
│  │     Expected Output:    The Finsys access requests page covers how        │
│  │                         access requests work and lists system specific    │
│  │                         access request requirements.                      │
│  └── Metrics                                                                 │
│       Status ┃ Metric             ┃ Score ┃ Threshold ┃ Reason               │
│      ━━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━  │

│  ├──   Input:              What is a true-up according to the quote to       │
│  │                         cash troubleshooting page?                        │
│  │     Actual Output:      A true-up is a back payment for a customer who    │
│  │                         has exceeded their licensed user count during     │
│  │                         the previous year. The true-up model is used      │
│  │                         to ensure the license never becomes a barrier     │
│  │                         to adoption for the client.                       │
│  │     Expected Output:    A true-up is a back payment for a customer        │
│  │                         going over their license count during the         │
│  │                         previous year.                                    │

│  │                         mission as:                                       │
│  │                         “To provide frictionless, world class,            │
│  │                         Application Support Experience for the GitLab     │
│  │                         Team members by providing easy to use Service     │
│  │                         Management tools, serving as a single point of    │
│  │                         contact for all Enterprise Applications,          │
│  │                         mitigating risk through compliance,               │
│  │                         governance, change and release management.”       │
│  │     Expected Output:    Enterprise Application Services states its        │
│  │                         mission is to provide a frictionless, world       │
│  │                         class application support experience for          │
│  │                         GitLab team members through easy to use           │
│  │                         service management tools.                         │

│  │                         our handbook is extensive and keeping it          │
│  │                         relevant is an important part of everyone's       │
│  │                         job. It is a vital part of who we are and how     │
│  │                         we communicate.”                                  │
│  │     Expected Output:    At GitLab our handbook is extensive and           │
│  │                         keeping it relevant is an important part of       │
│  │                         everyone's job. It is a vital part of who we      │
│  │                         are and how we communicate. We established        │
│  │                         these processes because we saw these benefits:    │

│  │                         company making suggestions to change              │
│  │                         significant things. Feel free to make a           │
│  │                         proposal, we can discuss anything. If we don't    │
│  │                         agree, we'll feel free not to merge it, and       │
│  │                         we'll thank you for caring about it.”             │

│  │                         agree, we'll feel free not to merge it, and       │
│  │                         we'll thank you for caring about it.              │

│  │                         experience and architecture of the GitLab         │
│  │                         Handbook is a shared responsibility. Feedback     │
│  │                         and questions can be directed to the handbook     │

│  │                         GitLab's product direction, strategy, and         │
│  │                         vision are documented in the handbook             │
│  │                         alongside product and engineering processes.      │
│  │                         This documentation allows current users to        │
│  │                         gain insight into GitLab's future priorities      │
│  │                         and helps potential users make an informed        │
│  │                         decision about adopting GitLab as a tool.         │
│  │     Expected Output:    Current and Potential Users: GitLab's product     │
│  │                         direction, strategy, and vision are documented    │
│  │                         in the handbook alongside our product and         │
│  │                         engineering processes. This allows current        │
│  │                         users a glimpse into GitLab's future              │
│  │                         priorities and can help potential users make      │
│  │                         an informed decision related to adopting          │
│  │                         GitLab as a tool.                                 │

│  │                         that this page is specifically about the          │
│  │                         editing process, while directing readers to       │
│  │                         the (../handbook-usage.md) for information on     │
│  │                         *how and why* the handbook is used, and to        │
│  │                         other (https://handbook.gitlab.com/docs/) for     │
│  │                         further reference. It also states that this       │
│  │                         page is intended to be complementary to [Using    │
│  │                         GitLab at GitLab](/handbook/people-group).        │

│  ├──   Input:              What does the Editing the Handbook page say       │
│  │                         about Tip Use AI to edit the handbook faster,     │
│  │                         and learn about Anthropic Claude and GitLab       │
│  │                         Duo?                                              │
│  │     Actual Output:      The "Editing the Handbook" page includes the      │
│  │                         tip: **"Use AI to edit the handbook faster,       │
│  │                         and learn about [Anthropic                        │
│  │                         Claude](/handbook/tools-and-tips/ai/claude/)      │
│  │                         and [GitLab                                       │
│  │                         Duo](/handbook/tools-and-tips/ai/gitlab-duo/)…    │
│  │                         This tip appears in at least two sections of      │
│  │                         the page — once in the introductory paragraph     │
│  │                         ([1]) and again in the "Practical Handbook        │
│  │                         Editing Tips" section ([2]), indicating it is     │
│  │                         a recommended, cross-cutting practice for         │
│  │                         accelerating and improving handbook editing.      │
│  │     Expected Output:    Tip Use AI to edit the handbook faster, and       │
│  │                         learn about Anthropic Claude and GitLab Duo.      │

│  │                         contains instructions, tips, and historical       │
│  │                         recordings for non-technical team members on      │
│  │                         how to work handbook-first. It serves as a        │
│  │                         supplement to the main editing handbook page      │
│  │                         (_index.md), which covers the basics of           │
│  │                         editing the handbook—including how to get         │
│  │                         help.                                             │

│  │                         historical recordings for non-technical team      │

│  │                         be moved once the meeting is over.                │

│  │                         always up to date, and others are better able     │
│  │                         to contribute.                                    │

│                                                                              │
│  ✅ test_case_54                                                             │
│  ├──   Input:              What does the GitLab Handbook Usage page say      │
│  │                         about When asked during an INSEAD case study      │
│  │                         interview (shown above) about challenges          │
│  │                         related to?                                       │
│  │     Actual Output:      When asked during an INSEAD case study            │
│  │                         interview about challenges related to being       │
│  │                         all-remote, GitLab co-founder Sid Sijbrandij      │
│  │                         stated that “the biggest problem is GitLab not    │
│  │                         working handbook first.” He emphasized that       │
│  │                         GitLab has an amazing handbook that enables       │
│  │                         collaboration, onboarding of new people, and      │
│  │                         collective thinking—but noted that it is          │
│  │                         counterintuitive to communicate changes to the    │
│  │                         handbook.                                         │
│  │     Expected Output:    When asked during an INSEAD case study            │
│  │                         interview (shown above) about challenges          │
│  │                         related to being all-remote, GitLab co-founder    │
│  │                         Sid Sijbrandij provided the following reply.      │

│  ✅ test_case_56                                                             │
│  ├──   Input:              What does the Content Websites page say about     │
│  │                         Therefore, we are using the term "content         │
│  │                         websites" here to avoid ambiguity and properly    │
│  │                         f?                                                │
│  │     Actual Output:      The Content Websites page states: “Therefore,     │
│  │                         we are using the term 'content websites' here     │
│  │                         to avoid ambiguity and properly frame             │
│  │                         discussions around this scope of                  │
│  │                         responsibility.” This reflects a deliberate       │
│  │                         shift from the traditional label *"the            │
│  │                         handbook"* to a broader, more accurate            │
│  │                         term—“content websites”—to encompass the          │
│  │                         expanded scope, which now includes multiple       │
│  │                         sites, projects, repositories, and types of       │
│  │                         content (e.g., the public                         │
│  │                         `handbook.gitlab.com`, the Internal Handbook      │
│  │                         at `internal.gitlab.com`, the job description     │
│  │                         library, and TeamOps pages).                      │
│  │     Expected Output:    Therefore, we are using the term "content         │
│  │                         websites" here to avoid ambiguity and properly    │
│  │                         frame discussions around this scope of            │
│  │                         responsibility.                                   │
